does: This paper evaluates the robustness of state-of-the-art LLM defenses against multi-turn human jailbreaks rather than single-turn automated attacks. The researchers conduct extensive red teaming with human adversaries across dozens of commercial engagements, demonstrating that models with reported single-digit attack success rates on automated benchmarks achieve over 70% ASR when attacked through multi-turn conversations that exploit dialogue context and iterative refinement. The work also shows vulnerabilities in machine unlearning defenses, where adversaries can recover supposedly-forgotten dual-use biosecurity knowledge through carefully crafted multi-turn interactions. The authors release Multi-Turn Human Jailbreaks (MHJ), a dataset of 2,912 prompts spanning 537 distinct jailbreak strategies, plus a compendium of tactics from professional red teaming campaigns.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research on adversarial robustness in LLMs and limitations of existing defense mechanisms. It extends prior single-turn attack frameworks like HarmBench and AutoDAN by introducing the more realistic multi-turn threat model, which mirrors actual adversarial behavior where humans exploit conversation history and iteratively probe model boundaries. The paper relates to concurrent work on safety evaluation methodologies that have shown single-turn metrics fail to capture real vulnerabilities. It also connects to research on machine unlearning defenses in LLMs, demonstrating that such approaches remain vulnerable to adaptive adversaries. The work responds to a gap between how LLMs are evaluated in controlled settings versus their actual deployment risks, and complements human-centered security research showing that human ingenuity often outpaces automated attack generation.
enables: This work establishes multi-turn human jailbreaking as a necessary benchmark for evaluating LLM safety, pushing the community toward more realistic threat models in defense development. The MHJ dataset and published jailbreak tactics provide concrete resources for researchers to design defenses that account for conversation history, iterative adaptation, and human reasoning. The findings on unlearning vulnerabilities motivate research into defense mechanisms robust against multi-turn information recovery attacks. Future work can build on these insights to develop training methods, architectural changes, or prompting strategies that maintain security across extended conversations. The paper also enables better understanding of which defense approaches have fundamental limitations versus which merely underperform under realistic conditions, informing whether incremental improvements or paradigm shifts are needed for genuinely robust LLMs.
